[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The picture?  I'm working my way through photos of my lineage, focusing on the teachers whose work has influenced me. The last two weeks were devoted to the leaders of the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor (H.B. of L.), one of the sources from which the Rosicrucian teacher Sylvester Gould got his training and background. Behind T.H. Burgoyne and Peter Davidson, who ran the order, was the remarkable figure of Max Théon, who's this week's honoree. His real name was Louis Maximilian Bimstein, and yes, if I had that name I'd probably call myself something like Max Théon too. Born in what is now Poland and was then part of the Russian Empire, he studied occultism from Polish Cabalists, then moved to London and helped get the H.B. of L. going. By the time that folded he and his English wife, née Mary Chrystine Woodroffe Ware, had already relocated to Paris, and moved from there to Algeria, where they spent the rest of their lives teaching an abstruse occult system, the Philosophie Cosmique. Mira Alfassa -- later The Mother, Sri Aurobindo's fellow-teacher -- was one of Théon's students in that phase of his career.

comments.title: Idhunn in Myth and Poetry, A Year with the Dead, and Bells in Ritual
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Thanks very much JMG for the Essene Apprentice Attunement - I didn't feel anything particularly dramatic during/right after the attunement, but I felt a good connection with the divine power I named, and my dreams and meditation have been fruitful, so we'll see how it unfolds.

To Share: 1) First, a book that will be most interesting to folks of a Heathenish bent: The Goddess Iðunn in Myth and Poetry by Maria Kvilhaug. Thanks to tamanous2020 and robertmathiesen for pointing me her way (I still haven't gotten into Seed of Yggdrasil, but I'm looking forward to it). I'm not sure I buy all of her conclusions, as she seems to have a few assumptions about the spiritual world I don't necessarily share, but she goes into a fantastic amount of depth, and most helpfully, she attempts to translate all of the proper nouns in the source poems, which adds a lot of additional fodder for meditation. I look forward to exploring more Gods and myths from her point of view.

2) Secondly, in keeping with my symbel boast for 2023, I've put up my second weekly blog post of the year, this time talking about how last year's goal of reading only books by the dead went: https://jpowellrussell.com/#thoughts_on_spending_a_year_with_the_dead. I'd very much appreciate hearing from anyone who chooses to read it, either by email or the comment thread on my dreamwidth.

To Ask: For JMG and/or the commentariat: any thoughts on the use of bells for prayer/ritual? Also, any books to read on the subject? Is there anything going on energetically/on other planes than the material, or is it mostly psychological/symbolic? In The Litany of the Tree of Life, I see that a chime is used, and the Survive the Jive video "Indo-European Prayer and Ritual" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Wzjx4yneCZs) features the ringing of a chime before a prayer/offering, and I've been wondering if/how I ought to incorporate something of the sort into my prayer practice.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for your wisdom and willingness to share it.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Bells in Ritual
replies.content:
The striking of a bell has several benefits. It is a well-defined event to signify a beginning, or an end to some aspect of the ritual. Also, the effective striking of some bells is not a trivial action, if the bell is to sound and ring to best effect. My (Lutheran) Pastor has a gong/bell, and when she strikes it: "boonnnnnnnnnnnnnng". When we had a guest preacher, who struck the same bell, it went "gonk". Clearly, the guest had not practiced and developed an effective technique, revealing a degree of casual overconfidence. So, the bell reveals something about the person who strikes it.

Lathechuck

replies.title: Re: Thor
replies.content: I'm going to turn this one over to my Heathen readers, who can doubtless point you to some first-rate literature on the subject. My only comment is that I've found Thor to be a very approachable god, less standoffish and less demanding than some.

replies.title: Re: Thor
replies.content:
Howdy,

For primary sources, the Poetic Edda and Snorri Sturlusson's Prose Edda are going to be your best bets - these will give you a solid grounding in the myths that we have good records of.

As you get into secondary sources, what you're looking for starts making more and more of a difference. Rudolf Simek's Dictionary of Northern Mythology is an excellent jumping off point for finding relevant primary sources and academic articles. For academic secondary sources, H.R. Ellis-Davidson's books are all fantastic, but her general intro to the Gods and myths is Gods and Myths of Northern Europe. I've also heard that John Lindow's Norse Mythology and Christopher Abram's Myths of the Pagan North are good, but I haven't checked them out yet myself.

If you want the P.O.V. of practicing Heathens, Galina Krasskova's A Modern Guide to Heathenry is pretty good, and I got a lot out of Kveldulf Gundarsson's Teutonic Magic and Teutonic Religion. The most classic book in this category would likely be the Book of Troth by Edred Thorsson.

If you're looking to branch out a bit and get some good themes for meditation, I haven't yet read Maria Kvilhaug's Trickster and the Thunder God, which I understand analyzes myths where we find Thor as friend/traveling companion to Loki. I've thoroughly enjoyed other books by her, and she gives a lot of thought-provoking suggestions, but maybe ground yourself with some of the other books before tackling this one, as she tends to speculate in ways that other folks find questionable.

Lastly, on the comics front, I rather like Walt Simonson's run on the Marvel The Mighty Thor, especially the "Ragnarok" storyline, which shows a decent knowledge of the source material adapted to the needs of the fictional universe, and more recently, the Thor: God of Thunder series has included some lovely art and fairly epic themes (even if not taken directly from the real myths). Oh, and Neil Gaiman's retelling of the myths Norse Mythology is available both as a novel and as an on-going collection of graphic novels with a variety of big-name comic artists, each doing a separate story (volume 3 comes out in March, I believe).

Hope these help!
Jeff

Oh, and P.S. As JMG said, I have also found Thor to be extremely approachable and helpful, and despite his sometimes reputation as a meathead warrior, a source of good advice, especially around the family and practical day-to-day issues.

comments.title: Linking Geomantic Charts
comments.content:
Hi JMG and Commentariat--

Is there anything in the literature of Geomancy about ways to link two Geomantic Charts that would otherwise not be thought of as related?

I did a mundane Geomantic reading on 1 Jan 2023 with the question:
"How will 2023 go for Canada?"
Mothers: Caput draconis, Caput draconis, Cauda Draconis, Albus. Here's a link to the house chart image:

https://amissio.net/gen/house/ee/74.svg

Cauda draconis in III and VI made me wonder about something ending with Canada's neighbour(s)--Probably the USA--that would involve skilled workers or possibly disease.
Via in VII and fortuna minor in VIII, X, XI may indicate a Black Swan event of some sort.

I tried to cast another chart with the question, "Will Canada experience a Large-Scale Black Swan Event in 2023?" Got Rubeus the first try ("Not now--busy!") and Cauda draconis the second try ("You already know the answer.").
That stumped me.
I looked back through the charts I had cast at the end of 2022, and found one with the same court as my Canada chart:

Question (on 28 Dec 2022): Will a nuclear weapon be exploded in 2023 that is not a test?"
Mothers: Puella, Puer, Puer, Caput draconis. Chart image here:
Rotated the chart so that VII becomes I and X becomes IV (An adversary affecting lands).

https://amissio.net/gen/house/db/be.svg

I had heard a news report that Mr. Putin was considering the use of nuclear weapons in the Ukraine war, and wondered if there would be a nuclear explosion, leaving open the possibility of accidental nuclear explosion, but restricting it to 'nuclear weapons.'
Result seems to be somewhat positive with Carcer traveling from I to V.

Since the courts on both these charts are Fortuna Minor, Fortuna Major, and Via, I wondered if Spiritus Mundi was linking the charts to indicate that the Black Swan Event may be a nuclear weapons explosion in Canada-- This would be very, VERY strange and unexpected since Canada has no nuclear weapons.

Feel free to delete or severely edit this comment-- I do not wish to cause a ruckus or draw the attention of the government watchdogs that be-- But I do wonder if Spiritus Mundi considered that s/he had already answered my Black Swan Question, and is pointing this out in the form of matching courts for the two readings.

Also note that I had a dreadful and solidly positive series of readings two years ago that did not come to pass, so hopefully this is also a false alarm.

Best wishes to all--
Emmanuel G/ Cobalt Choleric Gerbil

replies.title: Re: Linking Geomantic Charts
replies.content:
Um, no.

The single most common reason for inaccurate interpretation of geomantic readings is overthinking them. From my perspective, this is a good example of that! Since there are only sixteen figures, the chance that several figures will repeat in a series of readings on similar subjects is very high. Bringing meanings from one chart into another is a very good way to bollix up your reading.

Another reason for inaccurate interpretations is reading what you want to find into the chart. For example, how on earth do you extract a black swan event from Fortuna Minor in the 8th, 10th, and 11th houses in the first reading? Fortuna Minor is a favorable figure; it indicates transitory success, or success due to the assistance of others. The logical reading of that figure in those houses is that Canada will have a relatively successful year in terms of foreign trade and the activities of the government.

Those dreadful readings from two years ago? Go back over them and compare them to the events that actually happened, and figure out what they were actually trying to say to you. That's how you can learn to avoid repeating the same kind of mistake.

comments.content:
(Sorry for the length - what I consider most important is [1] and [2]; if I'm well off the mark regarding them, telling me just that and skipping everything else may be a quite good idea.) I've had for about 6 months a gorgeous coworker with very little in common with me. I do (think I) like a lot parts of what I know about her personality (definitely not everything - we seem to agree on little), and she (at least says she) has some positive opinions about me (not that I think this says she's been sexually attracted to me). The dearth of things of common is the main reason why I didn't try having a relationship with her thus far, but there's also the issue that she's been about to change jobs and probably cities for most of that time, which might've caused me to avoid anything I'd wanted to do regarding her. While she was never 100% absent from my thoughts outside work, it's been 2 or 3 weeks that I've thought so much about her that, frankly, I've been irritated, because at no point did I expect trying anything with her would lead anywhere that good. Later I noticed 2 things happened around that far ago that might be related: we had a work interaction that I thought showed one of personality traits I found appealing, and ... I stopped fapping - unrelated (AFAIK) to her, I'd been intending it before, and I only thought one thing might have to do with the other after I'd done the readings I'm to ask about, and "fixed it" (watching porn, not thinking about my coworker). (Maybe it'll help - I'm considering doing it less often than I used to, but not go 15 days without again.) I can't tell the exact thought process, but at some point I seem to have thought something like "I should ask her out - at least rejection might help me stop thinking about her [outside work needs] faster". And then I decided to divine about it - came up with 2 questions, then 4, then 2 (shuffled each time, should it matter).

[1] "If [coworker] decides to stay at her current job, and then I attempt to be with her, will the long-term results be good for us both?" [Translated this way because I intended both "date" and "marry" to be possible meanings.] - reversed Circle.

[2] "If I try to be with [coworker] now, will the long-term results be good for us both?" - upright Point.

I was only considering doing the first, but decided to ask the second for comparison. Given the fact that I'm pretty sure that I've made more mistakes through my life by inaction than by action, I initially interpreted them as "That'd be you not acting until the opportunity ceases to exist once again!" and "Do it already!". Later I thought the upright Circle might mean I should commit to *some* decision, and not a specific one, but then I thought I asked about a specific action. Should "good long-term results for both of us" be true, I decided to ask more. (I only thought later that I could've asked about results for each separately - but by now I'd like to hear from you about what I did get.)

[3] "Should I be with [coworker] for more than 1 year, what will be the worst problem/difficulty [I'm not sure which] for me?" - reversed Octagram.

[4] "Should [coworker] be with me for more than 1 year, what will be the worst problem/difficulty for her?" - reversed Right Triangle.

[5] "Should [coworker] be with me for more than 1 year, what will be the best part for her?" - reversed Sphere.

[6] "Should I be with [coworker] for more than 1 year, what will be the best part for me?" - reverse Square and Diagonal.

I don't mind [3] and [4] and at all - if I interpret them correctly, any long-term commitment has them - the worse being what anyone of any sense would expect isn't bad at all. But when the "positives" are half-WTF (I can't even tell whether the reversed Sphere means "the best part is one she can't figure out now", "[same, but nobody can]", or "her not knowing something would be the 'best' part for her" - squick!) and half-bad (I don't mind "consequences", but if it's the "best" part, how can things be not bad?). By this point I thought: doesn't look good for the long term, but the answer to whether I should ask her out still might be positive. In what sense? Maybe brief dating could have longer-lasting positive consequences - it's easy to think that, for me, just acting at all might start a welcome change of habits; no idea what the benefit might be for her (I don't think I meant, and that something designed by you would interpret, "good for us both" as including the possibility of good for one and neutral for the other). Thinking aiming at the short term may be not an awful idea, I asked more.

[7] "What should I keep in mind about me while attempting to date [meaning short-term] [coworker]?" - reversed Gnomonic Expansion.

[8] "What should I keep in mind about [coworker] while attempting to date her?" - reversed Continuous Proportion.

I have trouble interpreting [7] as either "taking my habits too far" (because trying to date is something I haven't done often at all - maybe "trying to date a woman without much in common with me", but I only tried that a few times in 2 decades, and I'm not quite sure I met a single woman with things in common with me that was single, out of not many in total) or being too ambitious (because compared to asking someone out expecting at most a short-term relationship of some positive consequence, I think I could've been less ambitious only if I'd already decided to do nothing at all), so I've no idea about that. comments.title: Flower of life
comments.content:
Hello!

The flower of life appears from time to time in a number of locations across the world.

Do you happen to know the lore behind this rather beautiful drawing and any references to pursue?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Flower of life
replies.content:
The term "flower of life" was invented by Bernard Perona aka Drunvalo Melchizedek sometime in the 1990s. It's a simple grid based on what older traditions of sacred geometry call the ad triangulum system, and yes, it makes a very pretty drawing. As an ad triangulum grid, it's also one of the frameworks on which some sacred sites and other expressions of sacred geometry have been based -- though there are other, differently organized grids, some based on that system and some on other systems.

These days there's a fair amount of material in print on the basics of sacred geometry -- I recommend Robert Lawlor's Sacred Geometry: Philosophy and Practice in particular -- but books beyond the basics are rare. I hope to remedy that; among my solstice presents last month were all three volumes of George Lesser's exquisite (and long out of print) Gothic Cathedrals and Sacred Geometry, which covers this in detail, and I have Frederik Lund's Ad Quadratum in PDF. But it's going to take a lot of study, and some serious time reviewing everything I've learned as a Freemason, to turn that into a practical manual of sacred geometry.

comments.title: Death and Reincarnation
comments.content:
Hi JMG
You may have spoken to this in the past but if someone commits suicide what happens to them in the afterlife and in a future incarnation?

From a karma stand point are conditions that the individual experienced in childhood and adulthood taken into account? Also is the current life's position or experience most likely the same spot they left before? Or are the circumstances random?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content:
1) I have indeed discussed this before, but it's an important question. Suicide's a bad idea from an occult standpoint. First of all, once you get to the afterlife, you're basically stuck waiting around until you would otherwise have died. It's very dull. Then, when you get reincarnated, you end up in exactly the same situation you killed yourself to get away from. That latter thing is what happens any time you run away from your problems; you have to face them and cope with them, otherwise they don't go away.

2) The conditions you experienced in childhood and adulthood are the product of the karma you earned in previous lives. If you want different conditions, you need to change the karma that you're generating in this life. There's never anything random about circumstances -- they're among the standard ways that karma expresses itself. If you want different circumstances, now and in the future, you have to change your thoughts, words, and actions, because those are what create and sustain your circumstances.

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content:
Not the OP

Is my own character also a circumstance? It's the thing that bothers me most, and seems most difficult to change.

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content: Your character is the other way in which karma expresses itself. Yes, it's hard to change, but changing it is the most important way you can work on improving your life.

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content: I'm sure you've written several times about how that works, but somehow I didn't get the point. If you don't mind to explain: how does Karma express itself in my character? Where do those traits come from, are they reactions to past life experiences?

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content: Your karma makes your character. Every time a person behaves like a jerk, let's say, the habit of being a jerk becomes more deeply engraved in his character, and other people treat him accordingly -- that is to say, they assume he's a jerk and cut him out of their lives, or go out of their way to make his life difficult, or what have you. That's karma. It carries over from life to life -- if you did a lot of jerk behaviors in your last life, you'll instinctively act like a jerk in this one, until the consequences slap you out of it.

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content: So - what was the origin of jerky behavior in the beginning? I guess this is a philosophical question as well, hmmm

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content: There are always situations where it's more convenient, in the short term, to act like a jerk. Souls that have just entered human incarnation don't yet have a clue about the downside, and so in any given situation, some of them will act like jerks. If that succeeds in getting them what they want, they go on acting like jerks, and become jerks. Other people then copy them. Meanwhile still other people are making their own mistakes -- being a jerk is not the only way you can mess up, of course! -- and before long you've got a complete muddle where almost everyone has some bad habits and some good habits, and all of these are becoming part of their characters. Rinse and repeat, and you've got human existence as we know it.

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation
replies.content: JMG, does this apply to someone who is terminally ill and chooses to take advantage of an "aid-in-dying" law (i.e., chooses to self-administer a fatal dose of a medication)? If someone is already close to death and chooses to hasten the process, would the karmic effects be the same as for a more clear-cut case of suicide?

replies.title: Re: Death and Reincarnation

You're still going to have to deal with that pain at some point, but depending on what else you've done in your life, you may be able to break it up into bursts of less intense agony and misery.

Dear JMG,

I posted a question last week about the breath of acceptance - thanks for the reply and also to the commenter who introduced me to the concept of the theosphere. Both replies were very helpful.

Once again I'm unsure of the OS instructions, this time for the Law of Happiness.

replies.title: Re: Octagon Society: Law of Happiness
replies.content:
Hey, I am not JMG, but since I am also working through the same material, and have completed The Law of Happiness one time, I thought I'd try to walk you through my thought process, according to my understanding, I hope this is ok:

If I were you start by examining what is it that my dad do that made me sad. I'd then see what is it in my reaction to this situation do that lead to my sadness. Then, I'd think about an action I do that I love doing, and if I were to do it instead of feeling sad, I could adress the issue. Perhaps an example could be useful:

Why did I become sad when my father said he is never coming back? Well, it made me feel abandoned, and I was sad to think someone I care about might leave my life. I felt like I will be alone forever.

Now, I test this assumption: would I really be alone forever if my father left? Not necessarily, because I am sourrounded by people, and if I will become a person people would want to make friends with, I could always have people in my life, even if someone close to me abandones me.

Now, I already generalized the situation: "I have trouble dealing with situations when people I care about walk away from my life, when it happens I get sad thinking I may be alone for the rest of my life".

Now, what can I do instead of feeling sad at this prospect? I could find something I love doing that is done in groups, and go there. I will immidiately be surrounded with like minded people, and perhaps when people see me doing something I love, a new person will come into my life?

So, here is the full generalization:

The next time someone I care about removes himself from my life, instead of thinking about being left alone, I will choose an activity I love doing, and sign up for a club or class.

replies.title: Re: Octagon Society: Law of Happiness
replies.content:
If I may: this is a most traumatic event for a child and I understand that it is hard to find anything positive in it.

Did your father not come back? Then the best part of it is that you survived and became an adult who is willing to work on those issues (along with all other qualities you possess). You have the potential to look away from this terrible event, and grow ever more as a human being.

If he came back, you could be happy that you can learn not to be impressed by people's foolish reactions, bad as they were for you as a child, and still have a heart.

replies.title: Re: Octagon Society: Law of Happiness
replies.content:
Okay, good. You've taken the first step, which is to identify traumatic events from your childhood that made you sad. The second step is to figure out how to deal with similar situations in the future in a way that doesn't produce the same kind of emotional reaction. Of course you're going to be upset if somebody you care about walks out on you -- that's normal and even necessary. Your goal is not to let your grief and other emotions dominate your life.

So what would you do if something like that happened to you now -- not in your childhood, but right here and now? I can't answer that, because your emotional life is yours, not mine. If it were my memory that I was processing, I'd explore ways I've learned to deal with grief and sadness: by paying attention to all the sources of happiness I still have in my life; by recalling that people in the midst of emotional crises very often say extreme things they don't follow through on; potentially, by recognizing that someone who would say something that hurtful to their own nine-year-old child may not be someone you want in your life. But these may not be choices that work for you.

Oh, and if the best you can do is neutral, that's fine. The goal is not to be perfect. The goal is to improve.

replies.title: What a quagmire!
replies.content:
Dear scotlyn

Please let me share the story of my grandfather. He committed some severe transgression (cannot tell, not my story. but, AFAIK, no one died as a direct result) in his younger years, for which he endured the guilt for decades without talking about it. Somewhere around his 60th birthday he found the courage to go to church and confess to a priest in the Sacrament of Reconciliation.

Most often than not, the priest will impose some token penitence: some prayer or other devotional activities, some minor stricture in one's common habits, and of course reparations to those affected by one's sin. This case was not really amenable or fit for reparations, and it did cause enduring hardship and pain on others... so mere prayer would not cut it. So, the priest spoke the penitence: "For the time you remain on this Earth, you shall never taste salt again".

It was hard. He would never put away from his mind (or rather, normalize/rationalize) that deed for more than a few hours each day. Even when he was taking all the nutrition he needed to survive, the meals would never be the same again (just as the people involved would never enjoy life quite the same way again, even if they lived, or even thrived in some respects). He would never partake in the same food as the rest of the family, so in a way he was cast away... adrift... alone. Then, there was the issue that sodium is necessary to preserve life and he had to take mineral supplements to ensure he was getting enough of it under his new diet.

But through the final decade of his life, he gained in some sense part of what he freely left behind. After the adaptation period, he was able to enjoy food again, though not quite the same foodstuffs. He learned to appreciate other flavors and other ways of cooking. He could share meals with the rest of the family, even if he and we were eating different food; and we could all partake from the same dessert.

I really don't know, I was too young to pay attention to those things when he passed; but I like to think that on some level he learned deeper truths through this experience. He may have learned that it is not the same thing to remember and to own up than to despair and feel guilty, though all can be appropriate in their due time. Maybe he had a chance to talk to that people, of maybe he didn't. Maybe he found a way to bless them and serve them anyways. Not erasing the past, no... but rebuilding a different karma and a different future.

(Sorry for the double post, JMG, I accidentally replied to the wrong comment!)

A few options from the Germanic side of things that might work for you:

- Nerthus was a Mother Earth goddess worshipped by some of the early Germanic tribes written about by the Roman Tacitus. It seems very likely that she is somehow linked with the later Norse Njord and his children Frey and Freya, as Njord is etymologically descended from Nerthus, and elements of the worship of Frey and Freya are similar to the worship of Nerthus (for example, carrying around the idol in a wagon during a festival time). In Tacitus's description, there's a bit of a scary side, as the Goddess's idol must stay covered, and the slaves that cleaned it after the festival were drowned. I believe the later English earth Goddess "Erce", referenced in a medieval charm is likely the same Goddess, and under that name, I prayed to her for a healthy pregnancy and baby, and so far our second daughter is doing great. I associate her more with the biological side of motherhood.

- Frigg, Odin's wife, is sometimes called the "All Mother" to his "All Father", she is the queen of the Aesir. A very strong, regal Goddess, you might see some forgiveness in the myth of her seeking out everything alive and getting it to cry for Baldur to bring him back from the dead, with only one troll (maybe Loki in another form) refusing to do so. I pray to her for more of the social/behavioral side of motherhood and family

- I don't believe the myths record any children for Idun (sometimes spelled Idunna or Iðunn), and her role in the main myth we have about her seems to be more that of a maiden (Loki tricks her into getting stolen by a jotun named Thjazi, who might represent mortality/death, and while she is away, the Gods begin to grow old without her rejuvenating apples), but I have personally found her very much associated with forgiveness, life, and spiritual healing.

Good luck to you and your wife!
Jeff

comments.title: Good Morning
comments.content:
Good morning JMG!

I'm new to these forums but came upon them from your "unofficial" twitter account.

My question for you is this: As I change myself to be more in line with God's will, does the macrocosm actually change in accordance with the changes I've made within, or is it my perception of the outer world that's only changing?

To describe what I'm asking: does everyone's existence in this world begin in the world of opposites? To work the path back towards unity? and if doing "the work" to align, does the outer world close in as well towards unity? Or does this world only "unite" when my existence or another's ends the illusion of self? i.e. death or at-one-ment (not sure if these would be the same) with the environment and God itself?

I realize there are many questions here, but I don't yet have the understanding or vocabulary to articulate this in a simpler way. They are all a part of the same question... Feel free to answer them all or none or one.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Good Morning
replies.content:
Yes, the world changes as you change. Partly that's because "the world" is very strongly shaped by other people, and changes in your character will strongly influence the way that other people respond to you. Partly it's because all things, including "inanimate" ones, have life and consciousness, and respond to changes in you. Partly, it's because the Divine is always present in the world, and shapes things in response to our actions and insights.

That doesn't mean that life always gets easy for those who walk the spiritual path! In particular, it means that if you have unfinished business of any kind, circumstances will see to it that you have to deal with that unfinished business. The further on the path you go, however, the more clear it becomes that you and your circumstances are co-creations, and grow and change together.

replies.title: Re: Strange experiences after practicing SoP for several months

replies.title: Re: Athena
replies.content:
I always found myself drawn to Athena when I was studying the classics in college, but back then I didn't take polytheism seriously, and since I've taken it up, she hasn't much reached out to me, and I haven't much tried to reach out to her, given my more general draw to Germanic Gods and Goddesses.

That being said, I've always liked and respected her. One discussion of what unifies her seemingly disparate traits - war, the city state, weaving, and so forth - is, of all places, in Neal Stephenson's novel Cryptonomicon, which includes a Jungian/Campbellian style archetypal analysis of her and her myths. From that, I've always found it helpful to think of the domains she has charge over as linked by the Greek word techne, which is usually translated as "craft", but is perhaps more usefully identified as the root word for "technology". Everything Athena is about is how you apply thinking/smarts/wisdom to solving practical problems. You have wool and need to turn it into clothes? Weaving. You have neighbors that want to take your stuff and you don't want to let them? Effective generalship and fighting. You have a bunch of people living together that don't know how to get along? Wise and effective government.

In terms of practice, you might find the free ebook The Soul's Inner Statues by Kaye Boesme helpful: https://kayeofswords.github.io/soulsinnerstatues/index.html . Regular poster hwistle pointed me to this book, written by a Neoplatonist Classical polytheist about how to get started with and develop polytheist practice, whatever Gods or Goddesses you worship, but it's especially well suited to the Greek and Roman pantheons.

Cheers,
Jeff

One interpretation of the Divine Name-direction associations in the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram is East-Transcendent deity, South-Immanent deity, West-Active power, North-Passive power. Or one could keep the Cabalistic pattern, but associate Neptune with Kether and Uranus with a Chokmah, making the order, from crown to soles, Neptune, Saturn, Sun, Moon, Earth.

comments.title: Humoral medicine
comments.content:
Any westerner who has studied the history of medicine has been exposed to the horror stories about the pre-modern practice of medicine in Europe and the Americas. "Heroic" measures including copious bleeding, purging with toxic metals such as mercury compounds and prescriptions made up of 20-20 ingredients are assumed to have killed more than they cured. Before the advent of modern medicine there were reformers such as Samuel Hahnemann attempting to construct medical theories on principles other than the Galenic theories that prevailed. But one has to wonder why the Galenic theory become so dangerously debased and ineffective. The history book assumption is that the system had never been effective and, apparently, that our ancestors were too ignorant to notice. Bleeding people is right up there with belief in magic, divine right of kings and other irrational foundations of earlier society.

However, it occurred to me that starting with the discovery of the Americas and increased trade with Asia the conditions of life in Europe underwent extensive changes. Diet, for example, had been fairly standard for centuries. Bread or grain porridge was the staff of life, made from rye, wheat, barley or oats depending on soil and climate. Since grain is bulky it wasn't shipped far, so people ate what they grew; an Italian peasant was unlikely to taste oats from Scotland or rye from Russia. Cheese and pulses such as dried peas and fava beans were protein staples for the poor supplemented by fish and occasional meat. The upper classes ate more meat, either beef and pork or wild game. Without refrigeration vegetables and most fruits were also local. Wine or beer or other fermented beverages were the common drink, once again depending on locality. Starting in about 1500 this began to change--distilled alcohol moved from being medicinal to being a beverage. By the mid 1600s coffee began to spread into Europe; tea and chocolate were other new beverages. New foods entered the diet as well: maize (corn), potatoes, new varieties of beans, squash, sunflower seeds, tomatoes and chilies. Cane sugar was another luxury product that took a larger role in the diet as its cultivation spread.

In short, Europe underwent massive changes in diet over the course of 2-3 centuries. Some, such as chocolate, tea, coffee, sugar and hard liquor affected the upper classes more. Others, such as potatoes and corn entered the diets of the poor. Could it be that the medicine of the time was unable to keep up with ailments brought on by these changes and that the natural tendency to fight failure with more of the same led to the deadly excesses that are most of what we have been taught about the system?

I'm just putting these ideas out for discussion by those interested in physical systems of medicine (as opposed to systems that work more with energy). Does this make sense?


Rita

replies.title: Re: Humoral medicine
replies.content:
I am afraid there's not much time to talk about it here. Would it be out of order if we move this discussion to my blog? I have created and entry just here: https://homeopathic-meditations.dreamwidth.org/4051.html

Of course, if you want to host it yourself, please let me know and I shall take it down ASAP.

replies.title: Re: Humoral medicine
replies.content:
Hey Rita!

I'm also quite interested in that topic, it is a great question.

I think another factor might have been in education somehow. The stuff I've seen from humoral medicine points to a quite complex system, able to adapt to change.

But, if people aren't properly trained, then they're prone to just grab a handful of techniques, without the understanding of why something is done in a specific way in a specific circumstance.

I see that happening in the permaculture movement nowadays. The system is quite complex, has values, ethics, a whole conceptual framework, the basis of which is understand and imitate nature, in the way it acts where you live. But people (and some teachers) just grab on and teach the handful of practical techniques that appear as examples - a herb spiral, a mud oven, etc - and then the system doesn't work, since the techniques were never made to work in that context.

You never hear about the four elements balance, or the four humours in the body, or any other of the theoretical framework. Just about the bleedins (which are making a comeback, it seems they giving blood is a one of the few ways to way to get rid of microplastics in the bloodstream) and the Mercury, the techniques.

Ah, and regarding Mercury, maybe some misunderstandings coming from alchemy?

Just speculating, of course. And I wonder if there are any areas nowadays that are going thorough the same process that resulted in that far removed from reality way of practicing medicine.

Kind wishes,

Fox

replies.title: Re: Humoral medicine
replies.content:
That's a good point.

Humoral medicine also wasn't as much into bleeding as the later "heroic" medicine; in the Middle Age and Renaissance, for example, physicians didn't bleed patients -- that was done by barbers. Physicians prescribed herbal medicines, exercise, and diet and lifestyle changes. Here's a summary:

https://slate.com/human-interest/2016/12/the-regimen-sanitatus-salernum-was-the-middle-ages-most-famous-health-manual-how-does-the-medieval-diet-hold-up.html

If you want the original of the Regimen Salernum, the most famous medieval health manual, it's here:

http://www.godecookery.com/regimen/regimen.htm

replies.title: Re: Humoral medicine
replies.content:
John Michael—do you think that the factors I have mentioned may have reduced the effectiveness of the earlier system and led to the more extreme methods? I didn't remember to include the spread of newer diseases such as gonorrhea, syphilis, and various infectious diseases that we haven't identified which would have also caused physicians to try anything that seemed as if it would work.

Thanks for the information on actual Medieval practice—it seems that in popular histories everything before the mid-18th century gets lumped together as ineffective, superstitious and ignorant—and I fell right into that set of assumptions.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Humoral medicine
replies.content: Yes, very much so. Medieval medicine was well suited to a small, widely dispersed, mostly rural or small-town population with little travel between regions. Once Europe urbanized and became crowded, the factors you've listed made the relatively gentle medieval methods insufficient to deal with the increasingly severe state of public health -- and yes, infectious diseases such as syphilis, smallpox, bubonic plague, and so on overwhelmed the health care systems that then existed. That was what led to heroic medicine: a desperate attempt to find something that would save people in an increasingly sick age.

replies.title: Re: Humoral medicine
replies.content: While I agree that copious bleeding is a bad idea, donating blood periodically does have health benefits for some people, so bleeding might have had some benefits as long as they didn't overdo it: https://vitalheartandvein.com/blog/3-health-benefits-of-donating-blood/. I recently listened to a podcast on the BBC on ancient Greek and Roman medicine and one on medieval medicine, and they talked about keeping the "humors" in balance (if anyone's interested, they were episodes of a podcast history series entitled "You're Dead to Me"). It sounded similar to the concept of "doshas" in Ayurvedic medicine, with its emphasis on balancing a person's various mental and physical traits.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy question on Essene sponsor deities
replies.content: It's not very promising. I expect the next attunement to be several months away, so you've got some time to reflect; remember that you can always use a general term like "the Divine" if you have trouble finding a specific name you want to invoke.
